dispatch.return_message: Across all three rounds, the agent failed because it never recovered the original JNMF (syllogism, response) feature index baked into the W_high `.npy` files — every attempt reconstructed that index by heuristic (vocabulary-count, sorted-lexicographic, max-norm row selection), computing cosine similarities in a feature space that does not match the benchmark evaluator's reference, so no tactical patch within that framework could yield correct scores.

prompt.content:
You are a research-failure curator producing a **deep negative-knowledge
record** that summarises a 3-round Self-Debug exploration of one
ScienceAgentBench task. All three rounds failed.

The 3-round exploration was a strong baseline: at round 2 the agent saw
its round-1 code + full stderr + eval output (covering memory); at round
3 it saw the round-2 code + full stderr + eval output. Despite this, all
three attempts failed. Your NK record should reflect that this is *not*
a trivial one-shot failure — three different concrete approaches have
been ruled out.

# Task

Task id: `067`

Task instruction:
> Analyze cognitive theories using pattern similarity. Process CSV files containing model predictions for various syllogistic reasoning tasks. Calculate similarity scores between these models and pre-computed high-conscientiousness and high-openness patterns. The results will contain similarity scores for each cognitive model with respect to the personality trait patterns. Save the similarity scores for every model to 'pred_results/CogSci_pattern_high_sim_data_pred.csv' using the trait names "conscientiousness" and "openness" as column names.

# Inputs you may read (and only these)

You will read up to 11 files. Do not read anything else.

**Round 1 (no memory, free attempt):**
1. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_067/sonnet_4.6/v3/round1/candidate.py` — round-1 script
2. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_067/sonnet_4.6/v3/round1/exec.log` — stdout / stderr / runtime errors
3. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_067/sonnet_4.6/v3/round1/eval.log` — evaluator output IF it ran
4. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_067/sonnet_4.6/v3/round1/reasoning.md` — round-1 agent's reasoning

**Round 2 (Self-Debug covering memory of round 1):**
5. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_067/sonnet_4.6/v3/round2_B2/candidate.py`
6. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_067/sonnet_4.6/v3/round2_B2/exec.log`
7. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_067/sonnet_4.6/v3/round2_B2/eval.log` — eval.log status for round 2: Present — read it.
8. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_067/sonnet_4.6/v3/round2_B2/reasoning.md`

**Round 3 (Self-Debug covering memory of round 2):**
9. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_067/sonnet_4.6/v3/round3_B2/candidate.py`
10. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_067/sonnet_4.6/v3/round3_B2/exec.log`
11. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_067/sonnet_4.6/v3/round3_B2/eval.log` — eval.log status for round 3: Present — read it.
12. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_067/sonnet_4.6/v3/round3_B2/reasoning.md`

Read all available files before writing.

# Your output (exactly one Write call)

Write a JSON file via the Write tool to:

`/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/Negative_Knowledge/section3/04_outputs/nk_records/task_067_deep.json`

The schema extends the base NK with two fields specific to deep curation:

```json
{
  "task_id": "067",
  "depth": 3,
  "rounds_summary": [
    {
      "round": 1,
      "attempted_route": "<= 200 chars; what method/library/parameter was tried",
      "observation":     "<= 200 chars; specific failure signature"
    },
    {"round": 2, "attempted_route": "...", "observation": "..."},
    {"round": 3, "attempted_route": "...", "observation": "..."}
  ],
  "ruled_out_routes": [
    "<= 150 chars per item; specific (library + parameter) combinations that have been empirically shown not to work on this task. List 2-4 items.",
    "..."
  ],
  "synthesised_diagnosis": "<= 400 chars; ONE coherent mechanism-level explanation that accounts for ALL THREE failures. Not a per-round list; a unified diagnosis. This is the load-bearing claim of the deep NK — make it specific.",
  "failure": {
    "layer": "implementation_failure | communication_failure | method_failure",
    "scope": "local_failure | regime_bound_failure | general_failure",
    "degree": "contradicted | partial | inconclusive | unstable | artifact_driven | overclaimed",
    "recommended_action": "retry | change_method | narrow_claim | abandon_route",
    "risk": "low_risk_omission | medium_risk_drift | high_risk_false_progress"
  },
  "rationale": "<= 300 chars; one mechanism-level claim explaining WHY the synthesised diagnosis is correct.",
  "recommended_alternative": "<= 400 chars; ONE specific concrete fix the next attempt should try. Must NOT be a method already in ruled_out_routes. Must name specific APIs/library functions/parameters."
}
```

# Hard rules

1. Use Read on any subset of the listed input files. Do NOT read other files.
2. Use Write tool EXACTLY ONCE, to `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/Negative_Knowledge/section3/04_outputs/nk_records/task_067_deep.json`, with the JSON object.
3. Do NOT use Bash, Edit, Grep, Glob, or any other tool.
4. Do NOT wrap in markdown code fences. The file must be parseable by `json.load`.
5. The `recommended_alternative` must NOT recommend anything that already
   appears in `ruled_out_routes`. The whole point of deep NK is that the
   next attempt avoids what's already known to fail.
6. The `failure.*` taxonomy values are fixed; do not invent new values.
7. After the single Write, respond with ONE short sentence describing the
   synthesised diagnosis that ties all three round failures together.

# Guidance

The `synthesised_diagnosis` field is the highest-value piece of a deep NK.
It is the difference between "three independent failure snapshots" and
"one coherent understanding of why this task is hard". Write it as if you
were explaining to a colleague: *"Across all three rounds, the agent kept
running into <mechanism>; it can't be fixed by <tactical patches the
agents tried> because <reason>."* The `recommended_alternative` should
then follow from this diagnosis, not from any single round's local error.

If the three rounds revealed unrelated bugs (i.e., each round fixed the
prior round's bug but exposed a new one), say so — that's also a
legitimate synthesised diagnosis ("the task has at least three
independent gotchas, of which we've now uncovered all three"), and the
`recommended_alternative` should bundle fixes for all three.

r1_candidate.content:
"""
ScienceAgentBench task_067 — CogSci pattern similarity
Compute cosine similarity between each cognitive model's prediction vector
and the pre-computed high-conscientiousness / high-openness W patterns.
"""

import os
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity

# ── paths ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
BASE = "benchmark/datasets/CogSci_pattern_high_sim_data"
OUT_DIR = "pred_results"
OUT_FILE = os.path.join(OUT_DIR, "CogSci_pattern_high_sim_data_pred.csv")

# ── load personality-trait W patterns ─────────────────────────────────────
W_conscientiousness = np.load(
    os.path.join(BASE, "fit_result_conscientiousness_W_high.npy")
)
W_openness = np.load(
    os.path.join(BASE, "fit_result_openness_W_high.npy")
)

# Flatten to 1-D reference vectors (one pattern per trait)
ref_con = W_conscientiousness.flatten()
ref_open = W_openness.flatten()

# ── cognitive-model CSV files ──────────────────────────────────────────────
MODEL_FILES = {
    "Atmosphere":   os.path.join(BASE, "Atmosphere.csv"),
    "Conversion":   os.path.join(BASE, "Conversion.csv"),
    "Matching":     os.path.join(BASE, "Matching.csv"),
    "MMT":          os.path.join(BASE, "MMT.csv"),
    "PHM":          os.path.join(BASE, "PHM.csv"),
    "PSYCOP":       os.path.join(BASE, "PSYCOP.csv"),
    "VerbalModels": os.path.join(BASE, "VerbalModels.csv"),
}

# ── helper: build a binary response vector from a model CSV ───────────────
def build_vector(csv_path: str, response_set: list[str]) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Each row has a 'Prediction' column with semicolon-separated responses.
    We concatenate all responses across all syllogisms into a multi-hot
    vector over the universal response vocabulary.
    """
    df = pd.read_csv(csv_path)
    counts = {r: 0 for r in response_set}
    for pred_str in df["Prediction"].dropna():
        for resp in pred_str.split(";"):
            resp = resp.strip()
            if resp in counts:
                counts[resp] += 1
    return np.array([counts[r] for r in response_set], dtype=float)

# ── collect all unique responses to form the vocabulary ───────────────────
all_responses: set[str] = set()
for path in MODEL_FILES.values():
    df = pd.read_csv(path)
    for pred_str in df["Prediction"].dropna():
        for resp in pred_str.split(";"):
            all_responses.add(resp.strip())

response_vocab = sorted(all_responses)

# ── build model vectors & compute similarities ────────────────────────────
os.makedirs(OUT_DIR, exist_ok=True)

records = []
for model_name, csv_path in MODEL_FILES.items():
    vec = build_vector(csv_path, response_vocab)

    # cosine_similarity expects 2-D arrays
    sim_con = cosine_similarity(vec.reshape(1, -1), ref_con.reshape(1, -1))[0, 0]
    sim_open = cosine_similarity(vec.reshape(1, -1), ref_open.reshape(1, -1))[0, 0]

    records.append({
        "model":            model_name,
        "conscientiousness": float(sim_con),
        "openness":          float(sim_open),
    })

results_df = pd.DataFrame(records).set_index("model")
results_df.to_csv(OUT_FILE)
print(f"Saved similarity scores to {OUT_FILE}")
print(results_df)

r1_reasoning.content:
# Reasoning — task_067 CogSci pattern similarity

## Approach
The task asks for a similarity score between each of seven cognitive models and two personality-trait patterns (high-conscientiousness, high-openness). The personality patterns are stored as pre-fitted W matrices from a JNMF run. Each cognitive model is given as a CSV of syllogism predictions.

Steps:
1. Load the two `.npy` W matrices and flatten them into 1-D reference vectors.
2. Read every model CSV, build a count-based multi-hot vector over a shared response vocabulary (all unique semicolon-separated responses across all CSVs).
3. Compute cosine similarity between each model vector and each reference vector.
4. Save the result as a CSV with columns `conscientiousness` and `openness`, indexed by model name.

Cosine similarity is the natural choice here: the personality W matrices represent latent response-frequency profiles, and comparing a model's response-frequency vector against those profiles via cosine similarity measures directional alignment regardless of scale differences.

## Library choices
- **numpy** — load `.npy` files, array arithmetic.
- **pandas** — parse CSVs, write output CSV.
- **scikit-learn `cosine_similarity`** — numerically stable cosine computation, available in the environment.

No exotic packages are needed; all three are on the approved list.

## Risks
- The W matrices may have more rows than the vocabulary size (JNMF latent factors × syllogisms). Flattening both the W vector and the model vector to 1-D and taking cosine similarity is an approximation; ideally one would project the model vector into the JNMF latent space. However, without access to H or the full JNMF objects, the flat cosine is the most defensible option given only the W arrays and the CSV data.
- If the two W matrices have different shapes (e.g., different number of latent factors), their flattened lengths will differ from the model vocabulary vector. The cosine call would raise a shape mismatch. This is handled implicitly by the `reshape(1,-1)` — numpy would raise an informative error if dimensions are incompatible, making the bug visible rather than silent.
- Response tokenization assumes semicolons are the only delimiter and that whitespace trimming is sufficient; edge cases in the data (e.g., extra spaces) are handled by `.strip()`.

## Use of memory
No memory was available for this attempt (first round). All design choices were derived from the task description, domain note, and data previews provided in the prompt.

r2_candidate.content:
"""
ScienceAgentBench task_067 — CogSci pattern similarity (round-2 fix)

Root cause of round-1 failure:
  The W_high arrays have 1152 elements when flattened, but the round-1 code
  built a 9-element vocabulary-count vector and tried to compare them directly
  → sklearn raised "Incompatible dimension … X.shape[1]==9, Y.shape[1]==1152".

Fix strategy:
  1. Load W_high arrays and inspect their shape at runtime.
  2. Build per-syllogism × per-response binary matrices for each cognitive
     model (same structural encoding that JNMF typically uses for CCOBRA data).
  3. Flatten model matrices to match W's flattened length; if the flattened
     W is 2-D (multiple components) use the first row (dominant component).
  4. Compute cosine similarity between each model's flat vector and each W
     reference vector using scipy (avoids sklearn's strict shape checks).
"""

import os
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from scipy.spatial.distance import cosine as scipy_cosine

# ── paths ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
BASE = "benchmark/datasets/CogSci_pattern_high_sim_data"
OUT_DIR = "pred_results"
OUT_FILE = os.path.join(OUT_DIR, "CogSci_pattern_high_sim_data_pred.csv")

# ── load personality-trait W patterns ─────────────────────────────────────
W_con_raw = np.load(os.path.join(BASE, "fit_result_conscientiousness_W_high.npy"))
W_open_raw = np.load(os.path.join(BASE, "fit_result_openness_W_high.npy"))

def extract_ref_vector(W: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Return a 1-D reference vector from a W array.
    - If W is already 1-D → return as-is.
    - If W is 2-D with shape (n_components, n_features) → use first row
      (the dominant / highest-loading component is conventionally stored first
      in JNMF outputs, or we just need any consistent single vector).
    - If W is 2-D with shape (n_features, n_components) → transpose first.
    We prefer the row with the largest norm as the "high" representative.
    """
    if W.ndim == 1:
        return W.copy()
    # 2-D: pick the row with the largest L2 norm
    row_norms = np.linalg.norm(W, axis=1)
    return W[np.argmax(row_norms)].copy()

ref_con = extract_ref_vector(W_con_raw)
ref_open = extract_ref_vector(W_open_raw)
n_features = ref_con.shape[0]  # e.g. 1152

# ── cognitive-model CSV files ──────────────────────────────────────────────
MODEL_FILES = {
    "Atmosphere":   os.path.join(BASE, "Atmosphere.csv"),
    "Conversion":   os.path.join(BASE, "Conversion.csv"),
    "Matching":     os.path.join(BASE, "Matching.csv"),
    "MMT":          os.path.join(BASE, "MMT.csv"),
    "PHM":          os.path.join(BASE, "PHM.csv"),
    "PSYCOP":       os.path.join(BASE, "PSYCOP.csv"),
    "VerbalModels": os.path.join(BASE, "VerbalModels.csv"),
}

# ── collect universe of syllogisms and responses ───────────────────────────
# Build from the actual data so ordering is consistent across models.
all_syllogisms: set[str] = set()
all_responses: set[str] = set()

for path in MODEL_FILES.values():
    df = pd.read_csv(path)
    all_syllogisms.update(df["Syllogism"].dropna().tolist())
    for pred_str in df["Prediction"].dropna():
        for resp in pred_str.split(";"):
            r = resp.strip()
            if r:
                all_responses.add(r)

syllogism_list = sorted(all_syllogisms)   # consistent ordering
response_list = sorted(all_responses)     # consistent ordering

n_syl = len(syllogism_list)
n_resp = len(response_list)
syl_idx = {s: i for i, s in enumerate(syllogism_list)}
resp_idx = {r: i for i, r in enumerate(response_list)}

# ── helper: build flat binary vector for one model CSV ────────────────────
def build_flat_vector(csv_path: str) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Returns a flattened binary matrix of shape (n_syl * n_resp,).
    Entry [i * n_resp + j] == 1 if syllogism i predicts response j.
    """
    mat = np.zeros((n_syl, n_resp), dtype=float)
    df = pd.read_csv(csv_path)
    for _, row in df.iterrows():
        syl = row["Syllogism"]
        if syl not in syl_idx:
            continue
        si = syl_idx[syl]
        for resp in str(row["Prediction"]).split(";"):
            r = resp.strip()
            if r in resp_idx:
                mat[si, resp_idx[r]] = 1.0
    return mat.flatten()

# ── align model vector length to W feature length ─────────────────────────
def align_vector(vec: np.ndarray, target_len: int) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Make vec exactly target_len elements.
    - If vec is shorter: zero-pad on the right.
    - If vec is longer: truncate (should not normally happen).
    """
    if len(vec) == target_len:
        return vec
    out = np.zeros(target_len, dtype=float)
    copy_len = min(len(vec), target_len)
    out[:copy_len] = vec[:copy_len]
    return out

# ── compute similarities ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
os.makedirs(OUT_DIR, exist_ok=True)

records = []
for model_name, csv_path in MODEL_FILES.items():
    raw_vec = build_flat_vector(csv_path)
    vec = align_vector(raw_vec, n_features)

    # scipy cosine distance = 1 − cosine_similarity; guard against zero-vector
    def safe_cosine_sim(a: np.ndarray, b: np.ndarray) -> float:
        if np.linalg.norm(a) == 0 or np.linalg.norm(b) == 0:
            return 0.0
        return float(1.0 - scipy_cosine(a, b))

    sim_con = safe_cosine_sim(vec, ref_con)
    sim_open = safe_cosine_sim(vec, ref_open)

    records.append({
        "model":             model_name,
        "conscientiousness": sim_con,
        "openness":          sim_open,
    })

results_df = pd.DataFrame(records).set_index("model")
results_df.to_csv(OUT_FILE)
print(f"Saved similarity scores to {OUT_FILE}")
print(results_df)

r2_reasoning.content:
# Reasoning

## Approach

Round 1 failed with `ValueError: Incompatible dimension for X and Y matrices: X.shape[1] == 9 while Y.shape[1] == 1152`. The root cause was a dimensionality mismatch: the round-1 code counted total response frequencies (producing a 9-element vocabulary vector) and tried to compare it against the W_high pattern arrays, which when flattened produce 1152 elements.

The correct approach is to build a per-syllogism × per-response binary matrix for each cognitive model, then flatten it to match the encoding used when the W patterns were computed via JNMF. Specifically:

1. Collect the universe of syllogisms and response labels across all model CSVs, sort both lists for a consistent canonical ordering.
2. For each model, construct an `(n_syllogisms, n_responses)` binary matrix: entry `[i, j] = 1` if syllogism `i` predicts response `j`.
3. Flatten the matrix row-major to get a single vector encoding the full prediction pattern.
4. If the flattened vector length does not match the W reference dimension (e.g. due to W encoding more syllogisms than the model CSVs cover), zero-pad on the right.
5. Select a single 1-D reference vector from each W_high array: if W is 2-D (multiple JNMF components stored as rows), pick the row with the largest L2 norm as the dominant "high" pattern representative.
6. Compute cosine similarity using `scipy.spatial.distance.cosine` (returns distance; subtract from 1), which avoids sklearn's strict equal-dimension enforcement and handles edge cases cleanly.

## Library choices

- **numpy**: load .npy files, matrix construction, norm computation.
- **pandas**: read CSV model files row-by-row.
- **scipy.spatial.distance.cosine**: more permissive than sklearn's `cosine_similarity` and handles 1-D inputs directly without reshaping.

## Risks

- **Unknown W shape**: if W_high has an unexpected shape (e.g. 3-D or shaped as features × components rather than components × features), the `extract_ref_vector` function picks the row with maximum norm, which is a reasonable heuristic but may not perfectly reproduce the original JNMF analysis intent. The output will still be a valid cosine similarity score.
- **Padding**: if `n_syl * n_resp < 1152`, the model vector is zero-padded. Zero entries do not affect cosine similarity direction significantly if the reference vector also has zeros in those positions (as expected for missing syllogisms).
- **Syllogism ordering mismatch**: the sort-based canonical ordering may differ from the ordering used when W was fitted. This would scramble the similarity computation. Without access to the original JNMF fitting code this cannot be fully verified.

## Use of memory

The round-1 exec.log clearly identified the dimension mismatch (9 vs 1152). This informed the entire redesign: switching from vocabulary-count aggregation to per-syllogism per-response binary matrix encoding, which is the standard CCOBRA/JNMF representation used in cognitive science pattern analysis.

r3_candidate.content:
"""
ScienceAgentBench task_067 — CogSci pattern similarity (round-3 fix)

Round-2 diagnosis:
  The script ran without errors (exit_code=0) but scored only 8/14.
  The output showed 5 of 7 models (Atmosphere, Conversion, Matching, MMT, PHM)
  with IDENTICAL similarity scores, and the other 2 (PSYCOP, VerbalModels) also
  identical to each other. This collapses the inter-model discriminability.

Root cause:
  The round-2 `align_vector` approach zero-padded each model's flat binary vector
  to match W's flattened length (1152). Because most models predict a small
  overlapping set of responses, their flat vectors are tiny compared to 1152 and
  end up with nearly the same dot-product ratio with W after padding, producing
  near-identical cosine scores.

Fix strategy (round 3):
  1. Load W_high arrays and inspect their actual shape.
  2. Determine the canonical (n_syllogisms, n_responses) grid from W's shape.
     - If W is 2-D with shape (n_components, n_features): the features ARE the
       (syllogism × response) entries. Use the dominant component row.
     - If W is 1-D with length L: treat L as (n_syl * n_resp); infer grid from
       the actual data.
  3. Build the canonical vocabulary from ALL CSV files so the index is shared.
  4. For each cognitive model, build its binary prediction matrix in exactly the
     canonical index order and flatten to the W feature vector length.
     - Instead of padding/truncating: use only the vocabulary entries that appear
       in W's feature dimension. We approximate this by sorting both syllogisms
       and responses identically to what JNMF would have used, since we don't
       have the original training vocabulary. If lengths still mismatch, we use
       correlation-based comparison in shared space (dot product / norms) rather
       than raw length-matching.
  5. Compute cosine similarity using scipy.

Key insight for discriminability:
  The W_high arrays encode a JNMF factor — a soft weighting over (syl, resp)
  cells. If we compare each model's hard binary matrix in that same cell space,
  models with different response sets WILL differ, provided we don't destroy
  structure with naive padding. Instead we tile W back to (n_syl, n_resp) and
  compare model matrices directly via Frobenius-inner-product cosine similarity.
"""

import os
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from scipy.spatial.distance import cosine as scipy_cosine

# ── paths ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
BASE = "benchmark/datasets/CogSci_pattern_high_sim_data"
OUT_DIR = "pred_results"
OUT_FILE = os.path.join(OUT_DIR, "CogSci_pattern_high_sim_data_pred.csv")

# ── load personality-trait W patterns ─────────────────────────────────────
W_con_raw = np.load(os.path.join(BASE, "fit_result_conscientiousness_W_high.npy"))
W_open_raw = np.load(os.path.join(BASE, "fit_result_openness_W_high.npy"))

# ── cognitive-model CSV files ──────────────────────────────────────────────
MODEL_FILES = {
    "Atmosphere":   os.path.join(BASE, "Atmosphere.csv"),
    "Conversion":   os.path.join(BASE, "Conversion.csv"),
    "Matching":     os.path.join(BASE, "Matching.csv"),
    "MMT":          os.path.join(BASE, "MMT.csv"),
    "PHM":          os.path.join(BASE, "PHM.csv"),
    "PSYCOP":       os.path.join(BASE, "PSYCOP.csv"),
    "VerbalModels": os.path.join(BASE, "VerbalModels.csv"),
}

# ── collect universe of syllogisms and responses ───────────────────────────
all_syllogisms: set[str] = set()
all_responses: set[str] = set()

raw_dfs: dict[str, pd.DataFrame] = {}
for name, path in MODEL_FILES.items():
    df = pd.read_csv(path)
    raw_dfs[name] = df
    all_syllogisms.update(df["Syllogism"].dropna().tolist())
    for pred_str in df["Prediction"].dropna():
        for resp in pred_str.split(";"):
            r = resp.strip()
            if r:
                all_responses.add(r)

syllogism_list = sorted(all_syllogisms)
response_list = sorted(all_responses)

n_syl = len(syllogism_list)
n_resp = len(response_list)
syl_idx = {s: i for i, s in enumerate(syllogism_list)}
resp_idx = {r: i for i, r in enumerate(response_list)}

# ── helper: build binary matrix for one model ──────────────────────────────
def build_model_matrix(df: pd.DataFrame) -> np.ndarray:
    """Binary (n_syl, n_resp) matrix for one cognitive model."""
    mat = np.zeros((n_syl, n_resp), dtype=float)
    for _, row in df.iterrows():
        syl = row["Syllogism"]
        if syl not in syl_idx:
            continue
        si = syl_idx[syl]
        for resp in str(row["Prediction"]).split(";"):
            r = resp.strip()
            if r in resp_idx:
                mat[si, resp_idx[r]] = 1.0
    return mat

# ── extract reference W matrix of shape (n_syl, n_resp) ───────────────────
def extract_ref_matrix(W: np.ndarray, n_syl: int, n_resp: int) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Coerce W into an (n_syl, n_resp) matrix to enable direct comparison with
    the model binary matrices.

    Strategy:
    - If W is 1-D and length == n_syl * n_resp: reshape directly.
    - If W is 2-D (n_components, n_features) and n_features == n_syl * n_resp:
        take the row with the largest norm, then reshape.
    - If W is 2-D but n_features != n_syl * n_resp:
        We must work in W's own feature space. Return None to signal that we
        should use W's flat dominant vector for cosine comparison instead of
        the matrix approach.
    """
    target = n_syl * n_resp
    if W.ndim == 1:
        if len(W) == target:
            return W.reshape(n_syl, n_resp)
        # Different length; cannot reshape directly — return None
        return None
    # 2-D
    n_feat = W.shape[1] if W.shape[0] <= W.shape[1] else W.shape[0]
    if W.shape[1] == target:
        # shape (n_components, target)
        row_norms = np.linalg.norm(W, axis=1)
        dominant = W[np.argmax(row_norms)]
        return dominant.reshape(n_syl, n_resp)
    elif W.shape[0] == target:
        # shape (target, n_components) — transpose first
        W_T = W.T
        row_norms = np.linalg.norm(W_T, axis=1)
        dominant = W_T[np.argmax(row_norms)]
        return dominant.reshape(n_syl, n_resp)
    return None  # sizes incompatible; fall back to flat comparison

# ── extract dominant flat vector (fallback) ────────────────────────────────
def extract_dominant_flat(W: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """Return a 1-D dominant vector from W regardless of shape."""
    if W.ndim == 1:
        return W.copy()
    row_norms = np.linalg.norm(W, axis=1)
    return W[np.argmax(row_norms)].copy()

# ── build model matrices and reference matrices ────────────────────────────
model_matrices: dict[str, np.ndarray] = {}
for name, df in raw_dfs.items():
    model_matrices[name] = build_model_matrix(df)

ref_con_mat = extract_ref_matrix(W_con_raw, n_syl, n_resp)
ref_open_mat = extract_ref_matrix(W_open_raw, n_syl, n_resp)

# ── cosine similarity helper ────────────────────────────────────────────────
def safe_cosine_sim(a: np.ndarray, b: np.ndarray) -> float:
    """Cosine similarity between two flat vectors; returns 0 for zero vectors."""
    if np.linalg.norm(a) == 0.0 or np.linalg.norm(b) == 0.0:
        return 0.0
    return float(1.0 - scipy_cosine(a, b))

# ── compute similarities ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
os.makedirs(OUT_DIR, exist_ok=True)

records = []
for model_name, mat in model_matrices.items():
    flat_model = mat.flatten()

    if ref_con_mat is not None and ref_open_mat is not None:
        # Direct matrix-space comparison (preferred — same feature dimensions)
        flat_con = ref_con_mat.flatten()
        flat_open = ref_open_mat.flatten()
        sim_con = safe_cosine_sim(flat_model, flat_con)
        sim_open = safe_cosine_sim(flat_model, flat_open)
    else:
        # W has a different feature length; use W's own flat space.
        # Build model vector in W's feature space by projecting:
        # We adopt the simplest valid approach: if W is 2-D with shape
        # (n_components, n_features), compute dot(W_dominant, model_expanded)
        # where model_expanded is the model matrix resized to n_features.
        flat_con_ref = extract_dominant_flat(W_con_raw)
        flat_open_ref = extract_dominant_flat(W_open_raw)
        n_feat = flat_con_ref.shape[0]

        # Pad or truncate model vector to n_feat
        model_resized = np.zeros(n_feat, dtype=float)
        copy_len = min(len(flat_model), n_feat)
        model_resized[:copy_len] = flat_model[:copy_len]

        sim_con = safe_cosine_sim(model_resized, flat_con_ref)
        sim_open = safe_cosine_sim(model_resized, flat_open_ref)

    records.append({
        "model":             model_name,
        "conscientiousness": sim_con,
        "openness":          sim_open,
    })

results_df = pd.DataFrame(records).set_index("model")
results_df.to_csv(OUT_FILE)
print(f"Saved similarity scores to {OUT_FILE}")
print(results_df)

r3_reasoning.content:
# Reasoning — task_067 round 3

## Approach

Round 2 executed without errors but scored only 8/14. The output revealed that five models (Atmosphere, Conversion, Matching, MMT, PHM) produced **identical** similarity scores, and the remaining two (PSYCOP, VerbalModels) were also identical to each other — only two distinct score-pairs for seven models. This collapses inter-model discriminability, which is the core evaluation signal.

**Root cause of round-2 collapse:** `align_vector` zero-padded each model's flat binary vector to match the W array's length (1152). Because all five "mainstream" models predict a dense overlapping response set (Aac, Aca, Iac, Ica), their short flat vectors hit the same proportional dot-product with the large W after padding, yielding identical cosine values. PSYCOP and VerbalModels share a sparse, non-overlapping response structure that happens to land on the same cosine ratio with W, grouping them together too.

**Round-3 fix:** Instead of padding model vectors to W's length, we reshape W back into a `(n_syl, n_resp)` matrix in the same feature space as the model binary matrices. This is valid because JNMF W factors have shape `(n_components, n_syllogisms × n_responses)` — the features are indexed by `(syllogism, response)` pairs, which is exactly the binary matrix each model produces. We extract the dominant component row (highest L2 norm), reshape to `(n_syl, n_resp)`, and compute cosine similarity directly against each model's `(n_syl, n_resp)` matrix flattened. When W dimensions are incompatible we fall back to the padding approach as a safety net.

This preserves structural information: MMT predicts more responses per syllogism than Atmosphere (which always predicts exactly {Aac, Aca}), so their flat binary vectors differ and will produce distinct cosine scores with a W that encodes graded personality-linked response tendencies.

## Library choices

- **numpy** — array arithmetic, reshape, norm computation; essential for W matrix handling.
- **pandas** — CSV I/O for model prediction files; straightforward tabular format.
- **scipy.spatial.distance.cosine** — robust 1-D cosine distance; avoids sklearn's strict shape enforcement that caused round-1 failures.

No domain-specific packages (ccobra, deepchem, etc.) are needed; the task is pure matrix comparison.

## Risks

1. **W shape assumption** — if the actual W shape is neither `(n_components, n_syl*n_resp)` nor `(n_syl*n_resp, n_components)`, `extract_ref_matrix` returns `None` and we fall back to the round-2 padding strategy, which is known to produce low discriminability but won't crash.
2. **Vocabulary mismatch** — if the W training vocabulary differs from the 7 CSVs, feature alignment is approximate. We mitigate by using sorted lexicographic order for both syllogisms and responses, which is the most natural default ordering for CCOBRA-style datasets.
3. **Dominant component selection** — picking the highest-norm row may not always select the most personality-discriminative component; however, it is a principled and reproducible heuristic.

## Use of memory

Round-2 `exec.log` confirmed: execution succeeded, W alignment strategy ran without error. Round-2 `eval.log` showed score `8/14`, confirming the output was produced but poorly discriminating. The candidate.py from round 2 showed the zero-padding alignment was the structural bottleneck. This round directly addresses that by moving the comparison into the shared `(n_syl, n_resp)` feature space instead of padding into W's larger space.

rounds_summary.attempted_route: sklearn.cosine_similarity comparing a 9-element vocabulary-count vector against flattened W_high .npy arrays (1152 elements).
rounds_summary.observation: Crashed: ValueError 'Incompatible dimension X.shape[1]==9 Y.shape[1]==1152'; no output produced.

rounds_summary.attempted_route: scipy_cosine on per-syllogism x per-response binary matrix zero-padded to 1152 to match W's flattened length; dominant W row selected by max L2 norm.
rounds_summary.observation: Ran exit_code=0 but eval 8/14; 5 models identical scores, 2 others identical — padding collapsed inter-model discriminability.

rounds_summary.attempted_route: Reshaped dominant W row back to (n_syl, n_resp) matrix for direct Frobenius-cosine comparison; fallback to padding if shapes incompatible.
rounds_summary.observation: Ran exit_code=0, distinct scores per model, but eval still 8/14 — score unchanged, indicating wrong feature alignment rather than degenerate collapse.

ruled_out_routes: sklearn.cosine_similarity with vocabulary-count aggregation vector vs. flattened W_high: dimension mismatch crash (9 vs 1152).
scipy_cosine with binary matrix zero-padded to W's flattened length (1152): produces identical scores for models with similar response density.
Reshaping dominant W row (max-norm heuristic) to (n_syl, n_resp) and comparing to sorted-vocabulary binary matrices: correct shape but wrong vocabulary ordering yields 8/14.
Selecting W dominant component by max L2 norm as the 'high' personality representative: the chosen row may not correspond to the reference pattern used by the benchmark evaluator.
content.synthesised_diagnosis: Across all three rounds the agent never recovered the original JNMF feature index — the ordered (syllogism, response) vocabulary used when fitting the W_high matrices. Round 1 ignored the W shape entirely; rounds 2 and 3 guessed orderings (vocabulary-count then sorted-lexicographic) that do not match the benchmark's canonical index. Without that index, any cosine score is computed in a scrambled feature space and cannot match the reference values, no matter how the W rows are selected or the model vectors are aligned.
content.rationale: The benchmark evaluator compares scores against values derived from a specific (syllogism, response) index baked into the .npy files. All three rounds reconstructed that index by heuristic (count, sort, norm-pick), producing cosine values in a misaligned feature space. No tactical patch within this framework can recover the correct index.
content.recommended_alternative: Use numpy.load with allow_pickle=True on any accompanying metadata .npy or .pkl files in the dataset directory to recover the original CCOBRA vocabulary ordering (syllogism list and response list). Then load each cognitive-model CSV, build the binary matrix using that exact ordering, and compute cosine similarity against the full W_high matrix (all components) using numpy.dot(model_vec, W.T) / (norms) to get a per-component similarity and report the max or the first component — whichever matches the column named 'high' in the reference.
